title: Multi-regulator breach-notification SLA tracker (24h NIS2 / 72h GDPR / 72h HIPAA)
exclusions: Does NOT determine whether an event rises to 'breach'; relies on classification decided in the incident-management system.
evidence: Saved search 'breach_notification_sla' runs every 15 min and writes per-incident SLA status (against every applicable regulator) to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.33(1) requires notification to the supervisory authority without undue delay and where feasible within 72 hours; the UC measures elapsed time from classification to submission evidence.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.33 (Breach notification to supervisory authority) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.39.1: Multi-regulator breach-notification SLA tracker (24h NIS2 / 72h GDPR / 72h HIPAA).
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.39.1' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: NIS2 Art.23 imposes an early-warning obligation within 24h and an incident notification within 72h; the UC evidences both clocks for every eligible incident.

controlTest.positiveScenario: When an incident is classified 'breach' (per incident_register.csv) and no regulator-submission record exists 90 minutes before the applicable SLA deadline, the UC fires with a warning; at breach, it fires at severity critical.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Incidents not classified as breach, or incidents whose notification was submitted before the deadline with a valid tracking_id, do NOT fire.

dataSources: See subcategory preamble. — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
| inputlookup incident_register.csv
| where classification="breach"
| eval applicable_regs=split(applicable_regs,",")
| mvexpand applicable_regs
| lookup sla_catalog.csv regulator as applicable_regs OUTPUT sla_hours
| eval deadline=strptime(classified_at,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")+sla_hours*3600
| join incident_id applicable_regs [search index=notifications sourcetype=regulator:submission | fields incident_id applicable_regs tracking_id submitted_at]
| eval status=case(isnull(tracking_id) AND now()>deadline, "breach", isnull(tracking_id) AND (deadline-now())<5400, "imminent", isnotnull(tracking_id), "submitted")
| table incident_id applicable_regs deadline submitted_at status
description: Tracks every incident that has crossed the 'breach' threshold and reports on the notification clock per applicable regulator. Imminent breaches trigger a warning; missed deadlines trigger a critical notable and a board-level communication.
value: Most enforcement actions against supervisory breaches (ICO, DPC, BaFin) focus on notification timeliness. This UC turns the obligation into a visible KPI instead of a post-facto audit finding.
implementation: (1) Ingest classifications from ServiceNow / ES incident index; (2) maintain sla_catalog.csv with regulator → SLA hours; (3) ingest notification-portal tracking IDs; (4) schedule every 15 min; (5) integrate with SOAR to auto-escalate. Indexes required: `index=notifications`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=regulator:submission`.
visualization: Gantt of incidents vs SLA deadlines, single-value 'incidents notified on time (90d)', breakdown by regulator.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `notifications` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `sla_catalog.csv`, `incident_register.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('sla_catalog.csv','incident_register.csv')`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.33`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.33 (Breach notification to supervisory authority) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.39.1: Multi-regulator breach-notification SLA tracker (24h NIS2 / 72h GDPR / 72h HIPAA)." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `notifications` under `regulator:submission`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Ingest classifications from ServiceNow / ES incident index. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

• **Hint 2:** maintain sla_catalog.csv with regulator → SLA hours. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 3:** ingest notification-portal tracking IDs. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

• **Hint 4:** schedule every 15 min. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** integrate with SOAR to auto-escalate. Automated remediation must be gated behind a defined approval workflow; compliance controls that take destructive action without human review are themselves a governance risk.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Multi-regulator breach-notification SLA tracker (24h NIS2 / 72h GDPR / 72h HIPAA)," the aggregation summarises incident flow — volume per category, time-to-contain distribution, and resolution cadence are the metrics regulators ask about during post-incident review.

This search correlates the outer dataset (index=`notifications`, sourcetype=`regulator:submission`) with a subsearch on `incident_id applicable_regs` across the full search range. `type=inner` means the search only returns rows where `incident_id applicable_regs` exists in both datasets. This is the correct choice when the detection requires presence in both sources — a left-join would include orphaned rows that cannot be evaluated against the compliance rule.

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

Time-zone sensitivity: the `strptime(classified_at, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")` conversion assumes the source timestamp is in the Splunk server's local time zone unless the format includes `%z`. If `classified_at` is emitted in UTC by the source system but Splunk's search head runs in a non-UTC zone, the resulting epoch will be off by the zone offset and the subsequent `where _time > classified_at_epoch` comparison will produce systematic false positives or negatives. Always validate by comparing `strftime(classified_at_epoch, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M %Z")` against a known-good reference event before promoting to production.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.39.1: Multi-regulator breach-notification SLA tracker (24h NIS2 / ]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.39.1,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=notifications sourcetype=regulator:submission earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `applicable_regs`, `deadline`, `incident_id`, `status`, `submitted_at`. Run:

```spl
index=notifications sourcetype=regulator:submission earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table applicable_regs, deadline, incident_id, status, submitted_at
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `sla_catalog.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup sla_catalog.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.39.1: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Gantt Of Incidents Vs Sla Deadlines (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Single-Value 'Incidents Notified On Time (90D)' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Breakdown By Regulator (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We help you see when incident clocks are tight so you can meet notification duties even after hours.
